factions.name: The Blue-Collar Believers
factions.voice: Focus on the small business aspect—the idea of ghosts as a plumbing problem.

factions.name: The Gozerian Occultists
factions.voice: A small but intense faction that tracks the Sumerian architecture and Mesopotamian lore used in the script.

factions.name: The Anti-EPA Libertarians
factions.voice: View the film specifically as a political statement about government overreach.

factions.name: The Aesthetic Revivalists
factions.voice: Focused on the visual language—cosplay, decor, and Burton's German Expressionist-for-kids gamble.

factions.name: The Death-Bureaucrats
factions.voice: Readers of the film as a satire of bureaucracy, taxidermy of meaning, and the afterlife as a DMV.

factions.name: The Nostalgia Defenders
factions.voice: The community that chose this film in the 80s; dwindling but resistant.

factions.name: The Inversion Readers
factions.voice: Modern viewers who read the protagonists as the antagonists; protagonists' triumphs recontextualized as violations.

factions.name: The Performance Appreciators
factions.voice: A single undisputed faction: viewers who watch for Nicholson's performance and then move on.

factions.name: analytical
factions.voice: Dogville (2003) directed by Lars von Trier. Curated baseline artifact for metric calibration and systematic scoring initialization.

factions.name: analytical
factions.voice: Climax (2018) directed by Gaspar Noé. Curated baseline artifact for metric calibration and systematic scoring initialization.

factions.name: The Accretionists
factions.voice: Physics-obsessed viewers who prioritize Kip Thorne's math and the realism of Gargantua.

factions.name: The Transcendentalists
factions.voice: Users who view the love-transcends-time-and-space monologue not as a line, but as a literal law of the movie's universe.

factions.name: The Dust-Bowl Mourners
factions.voice: A faction focused on the Sci-fi Americana and the film's prophetic anxiety regarding climate collapse and scientific denialism.

factions.name: The V8 Purists
factions.voice: Obsessed with the mechanical idolatry of the film. They view the car as the true protagonist.

factions.name: The Civil-Collapse Theorists
factions.voice: Focus on the broken buildings and vacant roads as symbols of a dying social contract.

factions.name: The Stunt-Veritists
factions.voice: A faction that ignores the plot to focus on the reality of the production—valuing the film as a historical document of physical risk.

factions.name: The Pope-Worshippers
factions.voice: Divine (Harris) devotees who view the film as a sacred text of liberation.

factions.name: The Bio-Horrorists
factions.voice: Viewers who analyze the film solely through the lens of its bodily violations and real-world gross-out stakes.

factions.name: The Trash-Theorists
factions.voice: Academics attempting to apply Camp theory to something that actively hates theory.

factions.name: The Trans-Allegorists
factions.voice: Focused on the Wachowskis' personal evolution and the body-as-prison motifs.

factions.name: The Red-Pill Intellectuals
factions.voice: A faction that has decoupled the film from the directors' intent to use it as a political/social framework.

factions.name: The Simulation-Theorists
factions.voice: Users who treat the film as a documentary/technical manual for reality.

factions.name: The Map-Makers
factions.voice: The codes are literal; the film is a leaked document.

factions.name: The Apophenics
factions.voice: The film is a trap designed to mock the viewer's need for meaning.

factions.name: The Script Formalists
factions.voice: Film students and screenwriters who treat the movie as the canonical example of a perfect setup-and-payoff structure.

factions.name: The Temporal Nostalgics
factions.voice: Viewers who report a longing for a 1955 that never existed—the film generates nostalgia for a past no one lived.
